content.text: The Delhi Sultanate was established in 1206 following the defeat of King Prithviraj Chauhan. It saw the rule of five successive dynasties of Turkic-Afghan origin: Mamluks (Slave dynasty), Khiljis, Tughlaqs, Sayyids, and Lodis.

content.title: Expansion under Ala-ud-din Khilji
content.text: At the turn of the 14th century, Ala-ud-din Khilji expanded the Sultanate deep into central and southern India. He called himself 'Sikander Sani' (the second Alexander) and used plundered wealth to finance an enormous military apparatus.

Muhammad bin Tughlaq is known for ambitious but poorly executed schemes. He attempted to move the capital from Delhi to Daulatabad (Devagiri) and back, resulting in great loss of life. He also introduced 'token currency' using cheap copper coins, which led to widespread counterfeiting and economic decline.

content.title: Key Features and Challenges
items: Political Instability: Successions were often violent, with almost two out of three sultans seizing power by eliminating their predecessors.
Taxation: Non-Muslim subjects were often required to pay the 'jizya' tax for protection and exemption from military service.
Iconoclasm: Numerous instances of destruction of Hindu, Jain, and Buddhist temples, motivated by both plunder and religious ideology.
Invasions: In 1398, Timur, a Turkic-Mongol conqueror, launched a devastating attack on Delhi, leaving the city in ruins and withdrawing with massive plunder.

content.text: Established by two brothers, Harihara and Bukka, Vijayanagara grew into a significant force in southern India. Its capital, Hampi, was described by foreign travelers like Domingo Paes as as large as Rome and very beautiful.

content.title: Krishnadevaraya's Peak
content.text: Under Krishnadevaraya in the 16th century, the empire reached its cultural height. He patronized arts, built grand structures like the Vitthala temple (famous for its 'musical pillars'), and composed the epic poem 'Āmuktamālyada' on royal policy (Rājanīti).
The empire met a tragic end in 1565 at the Battle of Talikota, where a coalition of Deccan Sultanates defeated and sacked the city of Vijayanagara.

topics.topic_name: Resistance in the East and West

content.title: Kalinga and Mewar
items: Eastern Ganga Kingdom (Kalinga): King Narasimhadeva I repelled Multiple inroads by the Sultanate and built the famed Sun Temple at Konark to commemorate his victories.
Mewar Kingdom: Under Rana Kumbha, Mewar resisted later sultanates and built the massive Kumbhalgarh Fort in the Aravalli hills, featuring one of the longest continuous walls in the world.

content.text: Babur, a descendant of Timur, established the Mughal Empire by defeating Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat in 1526. His victory relied heavily on recently introduced gunpowder, field artillery, and cannons.

content.title: Babur's Impressions
content.text: In his autobiography 'Baburnama', Babur described India as a land of great wealth and artisans, despite missing the charms of Central Asia. He was a paradox—intellectually cultured yet a brutal conqueror who erected 'towers of skulls' from plundered cities.
Akbar, who became emperor at 13, expanded Mughal control while transitioning from brutal conquest to political stability. He is remembered for his policy of inter-faith tolerance (sulh-i-kul).

content.title: Shift in Governance
content.text: Akbar abolished the 'jizya' tax, entered marriage alliances with Rajput princesses, and appointed Hindu officials to high positions. He established a 'house of translation' to translate major Sanskrit texts like the Mahābhārata (Razmnama) and Rāmāyaṇa into Persian.
However, his earlier campaigns, such as the siege of Chittorgarh, were marked by extreme brutality, leading hundreds of Rajput women to commit 'jauhar' (mass self-immolation) to avoid capture.

Shah Jahan’s reign oversaw a flowering of arts and architecture, most notably the construction of the Taj Mahal in Agra, considered one of the world's great marvels. He also built the Red Forts in Delhi and Agra.

topics.topic_name: Aurangzeb and the Beginning of Decline

content.text: Aurangzeb (Alamgir) secured the throne by executing his brother Dara Shikoh and imprisoning his father Shah Jahan. His 49-year reign saw the empire's greatest territorial expansion but also its exhaustion.

content.title: Reversal of Policies
content.text: Unlike Akbar, Aurangzeb reinstated the jizya and pilgrimage taxes, banned music and dance in his court, and ordered the destruction of several famous temples, schools, and gurudwaras, viewing them as 'un-Islamic'.
Constant wars in the Deccan for 25 years depleted the treasury and strained the administration, leading to a rapid decline of Mughal power after his death in 1707.

notes.section_title: New Regional Powers and Martial Traditions

content.text: Migrating from present-day Myanmar to the Brahmaputra Valley, the Ahoms built a resilient kingdom. They used the 'paik system', where every able-bodied male provided labor or military service to the state.

content.title: Battle of Saraighat (1671)
content.text: The Ahom commander Lachit Borphukan defeated a much larger Mughal force by utilizing the unique river-and-hill geography of Assam and persistent guerrilla tactics.

Started as a spiritual movement by Guru Nanak, Sikhism evolved into a martial brotherhood (Khalsa) in response to Mughal persecution. Guru Arjun and Guru Tegh Bahadur were martyred for their refusal to convert and for standing by their faith.

content.title: The Sikh Empire
content.text: By the 19th century, Maharaja Ranjit Singh unified Sikh confederacies into a strong, centralized empire that spanned much of the Northwest and resisted both Mughal remnants and British expansion.

Maharana Pratap of Mewar became the face of Rajput resistance by refusing to accept Mughal suzerainty. Assisted by the Bhils, he conducted guerrilla warfare from the Aravalli hills for years after the Battle of Haldighati (1576).

content.title: Rani Durgavati
content.text: Rani Durgavati of the Garha kingdom (Gond) was a valiant queen who built a prosperous kingdom. She died on the battlefield while fighting Akbar's generals, becoming a symbol of regional pride.

The Sultanate introduced the 'iqta system' (land assignments for revenue), while the Mughals used the 'mansabdari system' (rank-based military maintenance using jagirs). Akbar's finance minister Todar Mal introduced an efficient revenue system called 'Zabt'.

India remained one of the wealthiest regions due to agrarian productivity and guilds (shrenis). The 'hundi system' emerged as an early form of banking, allowing merchants to transfer funds across borders without carrying heavy currency.

content.title: Temples as Economic Centers
content.text: Temples were not just for worship; they were bustling markets that funded trade and developed community infrastructure like irrigation tanks and chhatrams (accommodations).

glossary.definition: A territory ruled by a Sultan, a title adopted by many Muslim rulers.
The rejection or destruction of religious images or icons considered idolatrous.
A tax levied by some Islamic states on non-Muslim subjects in exchange for protection and military exemption.
Currency whose value is established by decree rather than the value of the metal it is made of.
An Ahom administrative system where able-bodied males provided service (labor or military) to the state in exchange for land rights.
A martial brotherhood of Sikhs established by Guru Gobind Singh committed to justice, equality, and defense of the faith.
A Mughal administrative system where officers (mansabdars) were assigned ranks and required to maintain a specific number of troops and animals.
A traditional written financial instrument used as a bill of exchange or to transfer money across distances without physical currency.
A style of fighting where small groups use their knowledge of the terrain to carry out surprise attacks and ambushes against larger armies.
The principle of acting for the welfare and well-being of all society.

summary_points: Between the 11th and 17th centuries, India’s political map was repeatedly reshaped by Turkic, Afghan, and Mughal invasions and the rise of powerful regional kingdoms.
The Delhi Sultanate established centralized rule in the north but faced constant instability and eventual destruction by Timur's forces.
The Vijayanagara Empire in the south became a peak of cultural and architectural renaissance before its fall at the Battle of Talikota.
Mughal rule under Akbar moved from brutal conquest to a consolidated empire based on political alliances, administrative reforms, and religious tolerance.
Aurangzeb's reign marked the empire's peak territorial extent but also its beginning of decline due to treasury exhaustion and religious reversals.
Regional groups like the Ahoms, Sikhs, and Rajputs showed immense resilience, using guerrilla tactics and martial traditions to preserve their independence.
India's economic strength was sustained through sophisticated agrarian systems, merchant guilds, and financial innovations like the hundi system.
The period was a blend of hardship and cultural creativity, resulting in iconic landmarks and a lasting synthesis of indigenous and foreign influences.
